title: Acute Porphyria
oneLiner: Acute neurovisceral crisis (severe abdominal pain, autonomic instability, neuropsychiatric symptoms) triggered by drugs (e.g., barbiturates, sulphonamides), alcohol, fasting, or hormonal changes; life-threatening if missed.

sections.Acute Porphyria: **One-liner:** Acute neurovisceral crisis (severe abdominal pain, autonomic instability, neuropsychiatric symptoms) triggered by drugs (e.g., barbiturates, sulphonamides), alcohol, fasting, or hormonal changes; life-threatening if missed.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Respiratory failure from bulbar or phrenic nerve palsy** – Single finding: **reduced forced vital capacity (FVC) or inability to cough/handle secretions** – need urgent ARTF/ICU input.
- **Hypertensive crisis with arrhythmia** – Single finding: **labile hypertension (systolic >180 mmHg) or tachyarrhythmia (especially sinus tachycardia >120) with no other cause** – may precede cardiac arrest; treat hypertension with labetalol (safe) but avoid diuretics.
- **Seizure from hyponatraemia (SIADH) or porphyrin-mediated neurotoxicity** – Single finding: **new-onset generalised tonic-clonic seizure in a young woman with abdominal pain** – do not use phenytoin or valproate (both porphyrinogenic); use benzodiazepines (midazolam).
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you’ll actually see):**
  - Severe, colicky, central abdominal pain (episodic, no tenderness proportionate to pain)
  - Nausea/vomiting (often bilious, can cause hypokalaemia)
  - Tachycardia, hypertension, sweating (autonomic overactivity)
  - Neuropsychiatric: confusion, agitation, hallucinations, seizures
  - Peripheral neuropathy: motor > sensory (ascending, simulates Guillain-Barré)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal: bowels sounds present, no guarding/rigidity (pain out of proportion to exam)
  - Neurological: proximal muscle weakness, loss of deep tendon reflexes, cranial nerve palsy (especially VII, X)
  - Skin: blisters/hyperpigmentation (only in variegate porphyria and hereditary coproporphyria — rarely present in acute crisis)
  - Urine: may look reddish-brown (port-wine colour) on standing
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Surgical acute abdomen** (perforation, pancreatitis, cholecystitis) – signs peritonitis, raised CRP/WCC, imaging positive; **Splitter:** *no guarding/rigidity + tachycardia/paresthesiae → porphyria, not surgical abdomen*
  - **Pancreatitis** – lipase elevated, not explained by alcohol/gallstones; porphyria can cause pancreatitis as complication
  - **Guillain-Barré syndrome** – ascending paralysis but no abdominal pain, autonomic dysfunction less severe; CSF protein elevated
  - **Addisonian crisis** – hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia, hypotension (porphyria usually hypertensive), short Synacthen test
  - **Functional abdominal pain / irritable bowel syndrome** – no autonomic instability, no neurological signs; urine porphobilinogen (PBG) negative
- **Splitter:** *Urine dipstick for PBG (Ehrlich’s reagent)* – positive dichotomises porphyria from all other differentials; negative means alternate diagnosis (unless sample light-exposed or delayed >60 minutes)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine spot for **PBG** (porphobilinogen) — use trace blotter or immediate lab transport (must be kept dark, cold, processed quickly). A positive spot test is diagnostic; a negative spot makes acute porphyria unlikely.
  - ECG (rule out tachycardia, ischaemia, or arrhythmia)
  - Capillary blood glucose (fasting/hypoglycaemia common trigger)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, UAE, LFT, bone profile, LDH, CRP
  - Serum sodium (hyponatraemia — SIADH or hypovolaemia)
  - Magnesium, phosphate (electrolyte derangements)
  - Blood culture if febrile
  - Urine and blood for toxicology (porphyrogenic drugs: barbiturates, sulphonamides)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal XR or CT (to exclude surgical cause — but beware radiation exposure if repeated; use ultrasound if low suspicion)
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain if neurological deficit (e.g., posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome)
- **Special tests:**
  - Confirmatory: urine total porphyrins, PBG, and porphyrin profile; faecal porphyrins; plasma porphyrin fluorescence scan (to differentiate AIP/VP/HCP)
  - Genetic testing for HMBS gene (if AIP suspected), but not urgent
  - Family screening once index case confirmed
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **King’s College criteria (simplified):** 1) Severe abdominal pain + 2) Positive urine PBG (spot or quantitative) + 3) Exclusion of other causes. Definite if PBG >10 mg/L (or >4-fold upper normal)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine porphobilinogen (PBG) test** – omission risks missing acute porphyria and attributing pain to functional cause, leading to fatal neurological deterioration; also urine β-hCG in all women of childbearing age (pregnancy may trigger porphyria and affect management)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stop all porphyrinogenic drugs** – review drug list against BNF safe/unsafe list (e.g., stop metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, phenytoin, carbamazepine, sulphonamides, barbiturates)
  - **IV fluids: 10% dextrose infusion** — first line: 500 mL 10% dextrose IV over 1 hour if not hyperglycaemic, then 1–2 L/day of 5% dextrose with sodium chloride as needed (correct hypovolaemia with 0.9% saline if needed)
  - **Pain control: IV morphine** (safe, titrate to effect) – avoid pethidine and tramadol (porphyrinogenic)
  - **Antiemetic:** Use ondansetron (safe), or cyclizine (safe) – avoid metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, domperidone (porphyrinogenic)
  - **Treat seizures:** IV midazolam (preferred) or diazepam – avoid phenytoin, valproate, phenobarbital
  - **Autonomic instability:** For severe hypertension (systolic >180 mmHg) consider labetalol IV (α/β-blocker safe in porphyria) – avoid ACE inhibitors, calcium-channel blockers (theoretical risk; use with caution). For tachycardia >140, correct pain/hypoglycaemia first, then discuss with senior.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **IV haem arginate** (if PBG positive, significant pain not controlled, or neurological progression) – dose: [check local policy] (typical: 3 mg/kg IV once daily for 4 days, given over 30 minutes via a filter). Pre-medicate with dexamethasone (safe) if haem arginate not available, consider cimetidine (IV regimen)
  - **Carbohydrate loading:** Continue IV 10% dextrose (if oral intake poor); if taking orally, give high-carbohydrate drinks/meals
  - **Monitor:** Strict fluid balance, neurology chart (MRC sum score), daily UAE/Mg/P, ECG for arrhythmias
  - **Pain:** Convert IV morphine to oral morphine (or fentanyl patch – safe; avoid codeine, tramadol)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Avoid triggers:** Written trigger list (drugs, alcohol, fasting, smoking, hormonal contraception, stress, infection)
  - **Referral to specialist porphyria centre** (e.g., King’s College London, St James’s Dublin)
  - **Consider elective haem arginate infusion** for recurrent crises – discuss with haematology/metabolic team
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 10% dextrose at 50–100 mL/h (adjust to blood glucose); 0.9% saline for maintenance if needed
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (safe in porphyria, but avoid if thrombocytopenia)
  - **Diet:** High-carbohydrate, low-fat; NBM initially if vomiting, then clear fluids up to normal diet as tolerated
  - **Observation frequency:** ECG monitoring for 24h, then daily; vital signs q2h initially, then q4h; neurological observations (MRC sum score) q6h during first 48h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Morphine sulphate** (pain) – IV titrate: 2.5–5 mg q15min prn, then oral: 5–20 mg q4h prn (safe)
  - **Ondansetron** (nausea) – IV/PO: 4-8 mg q8h prn (safe)
  - **Labetalol** (hypertension) – IV 20 mg over 2 minutes, then 40-80 mg q10-15min to max 300 mg (safe)
  - **Haem arginate** – dose: [check local policy] (typical: 3 mg/kg IV over 30 min daily for 4 days)
  - **10% dextrose IV** – loading 500 mL over 1h, then 1–2 L/24h (monitor glucose)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Cyclizine** (antiemetic) – IV/IM/PO 50 mg q8h
  - **Fentanyl** (pain) – IV 25-50 mcg q10min, or transdermal patch (25 mcg/h q72h)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Avoid:** phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, barbiturates, sulphonamides, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, ketamine, ergots, rifampicin, progesterone-only oral contraceptives (all porphyrinogenic)
  - **Use with caution:** rifampicin, ketoconazole, aldosterone antagonists
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Morphine: use cautiously in renal impairment (accumulaton of metabolites) – consider fentanyl
  - Ondansetron: no dose change needed in mild-moderate hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing pethidine or tramadol for pain** – triggers severe crisis and may cause respiratory depression and seizures; always use morphine or fentanyl.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Respiratory failure** (↑ need for ARTF/ICU; assess FVC q4h if motor weakness)
  - **Seizures** (from SIADH or neurotoxicity – treat hyponatraemia slowly)
  - **Autonomic storm** (extreme hypertension, myocardial ischaemia, arrhythmia)
  - **Acute kidney injury** (from rhabdomyolysis or vasoconstriction)
  - **Pancreatitis** (from vasoconstriction or severe vomiting)
  - **Thrombosis** (if II haem arginate extravasation – give via central line or filtered)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Persistent peripheral neuropathy** (long-term motor weakness)
  - **Chronic pain / headache** (even between attacks)
  - **Renal impairment** (hypertensive nephropathy)
  - **Hepatocellular carcinoma** (10–20% lifetime risk in AIP) – regular USS screening
sections.Prognosis: - 1-2 lines: With early recognition and correct management, 30-day mortality <5%. Untreated, acute crisis → death from respiratory failure or cardiac arrest in 30–50%. Recurrence is common; lifelong trigger avoidance reduces crises.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check a urine PBG BEFORE giving analgesia/sedation** – false negatives can occur if sample exposed to light or not refrigerated; use fresh, dark container.
- **Write “PORPHYRIA – check BNF for safe drugs” on drug chart** and highlight in notes – this prevents iatrogenic crises from multiple agents.
- **Give IV 10% dextrose early** (even if glucose normal) – it blocks δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase, shortening crisis.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT treat hypertension with standard antihypertensives** (ACEi, CCBs, diuretics) – they are not proven safe; use labetalol only.
- **Do NOT send urine for PBG and wait for result before starting management** – if spot positive, start haem arginate immediately if pain refractory; do not wait for quantitative porphyrins.
- **Do NOT attribute pain to IBS or psychogenic cause in young woman with autonomic instability** – always rule out porphyria first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 1) Recurrence of severe abdominal pain despite trigger avoidance; 2) Any new weakness (difficulty walking, swallowing, or breathing); 3) Confusion, hallucinations, or seizure.
- **Follow-up:** Contact porphyria specialist centre (e.g., King’s College Hospital porphyria service); outpatient review in 2–4 weeks for genetic counseling and family screening.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar + Consultant on-call) if:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or respiratory rate >24 (potential respiratory compromise)
  - New focal neurological deficit (e.g., bulbar palsy, limb weakness)
  - Seizure or GCS <14
  - Uncontrolled pain >40 mm VAS after IV morphine
  - Labile hypertension >180/110 mmHg despite labetalol
- **Call ICU if:**
  - GCS ≤12 or respiratory rate <8 or SpO2 <92% (potential airway/respiratory failure)
  - FVC <30% predicted or declining rapidly
  - Persistent seizure / status epilepticus
  - Need for IV haem arginate in unstable patient (ICU for central line and monitoring)
  - Any arrhythmia with haemodynamic instability

rawMarkdown:
### Acute Porphyria

**One-liner:** Acute neurovisceral crisis (severe abdominal pain, autonomic instability, neuropsychiatric symptoms) triggered by drugs (e.g., barbiturates, sulphonamides), alcohol, fasting, or hormonal changes; life-threatening if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Respiratory failure from bulbar or phrenic nerve palsy** – Single finding: **reduced forced vital capacity (FVC) or inability to cough/handle secretions** – need urgent ARTF/ICU input.
- **Hypertensive crisis with arrhythmia** – Single finding: **labile hypertension (systolic >180 mmHg) or tachyarrhythmia (especially sinus tachycardia >120) with no other cause** – may precede cardiac arrest; treat hypertension with labetalol (safe) but avoid diuretics.
- **Seizure from hyponatraemia (SIADH) or porphyrin-mediated neurotoxicity** – Single finding: **new-onset generalised tonic-clonic seizure in a young woman with abdominal pain** – do not use phenytoin or valproate (both porphyrinogenic); use benzodiazepines (midazolam).

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you’ll actually see):**
  - Severe, colicky, central abdominal pain (episodic, no tenderness proportionate to pain)
  - Nausea/vomiting (often bilious, can cause hypokalaemia)
  - Tachycardia, hypertension, sweating (autonomic overactivity)
  - Neuropsychiatric: confusion, agitation, hallucinations, seizures
  - Peripheral neuropathy: motor > sensory (ascending, simulates Guillain-Barré)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal: bowels sounds present, no guarding/rigidity (pain out of proportion to exam)
  - Neurological: proximal muscle weakness, loss of deep tendon reflexes, cranial nerve palsy (especially VII, X)
  - Skin: blisters/hyperpigmentation (only in variegate porphyria and hereditary coproporphyria — rarely present in acute crisis)
  - Urine: may look reddish-brown (port-wine colour) on standing

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Surgical acute abdomen** (perforation, pancreatitis, cholecystitis) – signs peritonitis, raised CRP/WCC, imaging positive; **Splitter:** *no guarding/rigidity + tachycardia/paresthesiae → porphyria, not surgical abdomen*
  - **Pancreatitis** – lipase elevated, not explained by alcohol/gallstones; porphyria can cause pancreatitis as complication
  - **Guillain-Barré syndrome** – ascending paralysis but no abdominal pain, autonomic dysfunction less severe; CSF protein elevated
  - **Addisonian crisis** – hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia, hypotension (porphyria usually hypertensive), short Synacthen test
  - **Functional abdominal pain / irritable bowel syndrome** – no autonomic instability, no neurological signs; urine porphobilinogen (PBG) negative
- **Splitter:** *Urine dipstick for PBG (Ehrlich’s reagent)* – positive dichotomises porphyria from all other differentials; negative means alternate diagnosis (unless sample light-exposed or delayed >60 minutes)

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine spot for **PBG** (porphobilinogen) — use trace blotter or immediate lab transport (must be kept dark, cold, processed quickly). A positive spot test is diagnostic; a negative spot makes acute porphyria unlikely.
  - ECG (rule out tachycardia, ischaemia, or arrhythmia)
  - Capillary blood glucose (fasting/hypoglycaemia common trigger)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, UAE, LFT, bone profile, LDH, CRP
  - Serum sodium (hyponatraemia — SIADH or hypovolaemia)
  - Magnesium, phosphate (electrolyte derangements)
  - Blood culture if febrile
  - Urine and blood for toxicology (porphyrogenic drugs: barbiturates, sulphonamides)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal XR or CT (to exclude surgical cause — but beware radiation exposure if repeated; use ultrasound if low suspicion)
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain if neurological deficit (e.g., posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome)
- **Special tests:**
  - Confirmatory: urine total porphyrins, PBG, and porphyrin profile; faecal porphyrins; plasma porphyrin fluorescence scan (to differentiate AIP/VP/HCP)
  - Genetic testing for HMBS gene (if AIP suspected), but not urgent
  - Family screening once index case confirmed
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **King’s College criteria (simplified):** 1) Severe abdominal pain + 2) Positive urine PBG (spot or quantitative) + 3) Exclusion of other causes. Definite if PBG >10 mg/L (or >4-fold upper normal)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine porphobilinogen (PBG) test** – omission risks missing acute porphyria and attributing pain to functional cause, leading to fatal neurological deterioration; also urine β-hCG in all women of childbearing age (pregnancy may trigger porphyria and affect management)

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Stop all porphyrinogenic drugs** – review drug list against BNF safe/unsafe list (e.g., stop metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, phenytoin, carbamazepine, sulphonamides, barbiturates)
  - **IV fluids: 10% dextrose infusion** — first line: 500 mL 10% dextrose IV over 1 hour if not hyperglycaemic, then 1–2 L/day of 5% dextrose with sodium chloride as needed (correct hypovolaemia with 0.9% saline if needed)
  - **Pain control: IV morphine** (safe, titrate to effect) – avoid pethidine and tramadol (porphyrinogenic)
  - **Antiemetic:** Use ondansetron (safe), or cyclizine (safe) – avoid metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, domperidone (porphyrinogenic)
  - **Treat seizures:** IV midazolam (preferred) or diazepam – avoid phenytoin, valproate, phenobarbital
  - **Autonomic instability:** For severe hypertension (systolic >180 mmHg) consider labetalol IV (α/β-blocker safe in porphyria) – avoid ACE inhibitors, calcium-channel blockers (theoretical risk; use with caution). For tachycardia >140, correct pain/hypoglycaemia first, then discuss with senior.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **IV haem arginate** (if PBG positive, significant pain not controlled, or neurological progression) – dose: [check local policy] (typical: 3 mg/kg IV once daily for 4 days, given over 30 minutes via a filter). Pre-medicate with dexamethasone (safe) if haem arginate not available, consider cimetidine (IV regimen)
  - **Carbohydrate loading:** Continue IV 10% dextrose (if oral intake poor); if taking orally, give high-carbohydrate drinks/meals
  - **Monitor:** Strict fluid balance, neurology chart (MRC sum score), daily UAE/Mg/P, ECG for arrhythmias
  - **Pain:** Convert IV morphine to oral morphine (or fentanyl patch – safe; avoid codeine, tramadol)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Avoid triggers:** Written trigger list (drugs, alcohol, fasting, smoking, hormonal contraception, stress, infection)
  - **Referral to specialist porphyria centre** (e.g., King’s College London, St James’s Dublin)
  - **Consider elective haem arginate infusion** for recurrent crises – discuss with haematology/metabolic team
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 10% dextrose at 50–100 mL/h (adjust to blood glucose); 0.9% saline for maintenance if needed
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (safe in porphyria, but avoid if thrombocytopenia)
  - **Diet:** High-carbohydrate, low-fat; NBM initially if vomiting, then clear fluids up to normal diet as tolerated
  - **Observation frequency:** ECG monitoring for 24h, then daily; vital signs q2h initially, then q4h; neurological observations (MRC sum score) q6h during first 48h

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents:**
  - **Morphine sulphate** (pain) – IV titrate: 2.5–5 mg q15min prn, then oral: 5–20 mg q4h prn (safe)
  - **Ondansetron** (nausea) – IV/PO: 4-8 mg q8h prn (safe)
  - **Labetalol** (hypertension) – IV 20 mg over 2 minutes, then 40-80 mg q10-15min to max 300 mg (safe)
  - **Haem arginate** – dose: [check local policy] (typical: 3 mg/kg IV over 30 min daily for 4 days)
  - **10% dextrose IV** – loading 500 mL over 1h, then 1–2 L/24h (monitor glucose)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Cyclizine** (antiemetic) – IV/IM/PO 50 mg q8h
  - **Fentanyl** (pain) – IV 25-50 mcg q10min, or transdermal patch (25 mcg/h q72h)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Avoid:** phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, barbiturates, sulphonamides, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, ketamine, ergots, rifampicin, progesterone-only oral contraceptives (all porphyrinogenic)
  - **Use with caution:** rifampicin, ketoconazole, aldosterone antagonists
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Morphine: use cautiously in renal impairment (accumulaton of metabolites) – consider fentanyl
  - Ondansetron: no dose change needed in mild-moderate hepatic impairment; avoid in severe hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing pethidine or tramadol for pain** – triggers severe crisis and may cause respiratory depression and seizures; always use morphine or fentanyl.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - **Respiratory failure** (↑ need for ARTF/ICU; assess FVC q4h if motor weakness)
  - **Seizures** (from SIADH or neurotoxicity – treat hyponatraemia slowly)
  - **Autonomic storm** (extreme hypertension, myocardial ischaemia, arrhythmia)
  - **Acute kidney injury** (from rhabdomyolysis or vasoconstriction)
  - **Pancreatitis** (from vasoconstriction or severe vomiting)
  - **Thrombosis** (if II haem arginate extravasation – give via central line or filtered)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Persistent peripheral neuropathy** (long-term motor weakness)
  - **Chronic pain / headache** (even between attacks)
  - **Renal impairment** (hypertensive nephropathy)
  - **Hepatocellular carcinoma** (10–20% lifetime risk in AIP) – regular USS screening

### Prognosis

- 1-2 lines: With early recognition and correct management, 30-day mortality <5%. Untreated, acute crisis → death from respiratory failure or cardiac arrest in 30–50%. Recurrence is common; lifelong trigger avoidance reduces crises.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always check a urine PBG BEFORE giving analgesia/sedation** – false negatives can occur if sample exposed to light or not refrigerated; use fresh, dark container.
- **Write “PORPHYRIA – check BNF for safe drugs” on drug chart** and highlight in notes – this prevents iatrogenic crises from multiple agents.
- **Give IV 10% dextrose early** (even if glucose normal) – it blocks δ-aminolevulinic acid synthase, shortening crisis.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do NOT treat hypertension with standard antihypertensives** (ACEi, CCBs, diuretics) – they are not proven safe; use labetalol only.
- **Do NOT send urine for PBG and wait for result before starting management** – if spot positive, start haem arginate immediately if pain refractory; do not wait for quantitative porphyrins.
- **Do NOT attribute pain to IBS or psychogenic cause in young woman with autonomic instability** – always rule out porphyria first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** 1) Recurrence of severe abdominal pain despite trigger avoidance; 2) Any new weakness (difficulty walking, swallowing, or breathing); 3) Confusion, hallucinations, or seizure.
- **Follow-up:** Contact porphyria specialist centre (e.g., King’s College Hospital porphyria service); outpatient review in 2–4 weeks for genetic counseling and family screening.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (Registrar + Consultant on-call) if:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or respiratory rate >24 (potential respiratory compromise)
  - New focal neurological deficit (e.g., bulbar palsy, limb weakness)
  - Seizure or GCS <14
  - Uncontrolled pain >40 mm VAS after IV morphine
  - Labile hypertension >180/110 mmHg despite labetalol
- **Call ICU if:**
  - GCS ≤12 or respiratory rate <8 or SpO2 <92% (potential airway/respiratory failure)
  - FVC <30% predicted or declining rapidly
  - Persistent seizure / status epilepticus
  - Need for IV haem arginate in unstable patient (ICU for central line and monitoring)
  - Any arrhythmia with haemodynamic instability
